reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: The 1997 Agreement between the Holy See and the Republic of Hungary on the financing of the Catholic Church's public-service activities, and the parallel accords concluded with other historic churches under Act CCVI of 2011 on the Right to Freedom of Conscience and Religion and the Legal Status of Churches, recognise denominational maintainers' autonomy over pedagogy, institutional character and admissions practice as the condition attached to state funding parity (normatíva).
reactions.interest: Church and foundation (non-state) school maintainers' institutional and admissions autonomy
reactions.public_good_frame: Institutional pluralism and subsidiarity: a diverse ecology of maintainers serving different communities and worldviews is itself a public good, and should not be treated as a residual category to be conscripted into state-designed uniform rules whenever it is administratively convenient.
reactions.argument: We take no issue with the state redesigning admission instruments inside its own maintained tracks. But the scenario's own mechanism explicitly proposes 'regulating non-state (church/foundation) maintainer admissions in parallel' to stop demand migration — this reaches directly into our accord-protected autonomy over who we admit and on what basis (including legitimate confessional criteria), and it does so by the state's own admission on 'weak' evidence, with 'no Hungarian precedent for enforced uniform admissions across maintainer types.' Imposing a uniform quota regime on independent maintainers by fiat, rather than by negotiated agreement, would breach the reciprocal basis of the church-state settlement.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would support participation if any admissions adjustment for non-state schools were reached through bilateral negotiation (not unilateral legislation or threat of subsidy withdrawal), preserved our right to apply legitimate denominational admission criteria, and did not treat non-state schools as a compliance afterthought to a state-designed reform.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Same church-state accord basis as S1: denominational maintainers' structural and admissions autonomy is guaranteed in exchange for accepting a public-service educational role, and the scenario's own implementation_steps concede that 'a single statutory act cannot bind independent maintainers by fiat,' requiring separate negotiation.
reactions.interest: Church and foundation (non-state) school maintainers' autonomy over their own enrollment capacity and institutional continuity
reactions.public_good_frame: Legal predictability and contractual good faith between the state and its accord-partner maintainers protect the long-term viability of a plural school system; unilateral capping of an independent sector undermines the trust on which that plurality depends.
reactions.argument: A statutory cap trajectory imposed only on state schools while non-state maintainers are merely invited to negotiate 'an equivalent or compensating' arrangement is not equivalence — it either leaves our capacity untouched (in which case we are being asked to solve the state's demand-migration problem informally) or it asks us to accept the same shrinking-capacity trajectory as a matter of comity, which affects our institutions' financial model (enrollment-linked funding) and long-term mission without the legislative process that would apply to state schools. We can only assess this scenario meaningfully once a concrete negotiated proposal exists.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would move toward support if any cap or compensating arrangement were the product of genuine negotiation with maintainer representative bodies, included proportional compensation for any capacity reduction, and preserved our right to decline without loss of existing funding entitlements.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Denominational maintainers' constitutionally and accord-protected right to determine their own institutional structure and educational offer, per Act CCVI of 2011 and the church-state financing agreements referenced above, which extend to school type and grade structure, not only curriculum content.
reactions.interest: Church and foundation (non-state) school maintainers' right to maintain their own institutional structure, including 6-/8-year gimnázium form
reactions.public_good_frame: Preserving a plural landscape of school types — including long-established denominational institutions with a distinct pedagogical and formational mission spanning ages 10/12 to 18 — serves educational diversity and parental choice better than a single mandated comprehensive model.
reactions.argument: This scenario abolishes the 6-/8-year entry point as a structural category, which for many church-maintained gimnáziums is not an incidental admissions channel but the institutional form itself, built around a continuous formation model. The scenario's own political-risk analysis names church/private maintainer resistance as a driver of 'the highest-intensity opposition of all four scenarios' — that is a description of exactly what we would do, and it reflects genuine loss of institutional self-determination, not simply preference for the status quo. The scenario also offers no described fallback or opt-out for maintainers if a national pact cannot be secured, which by its own account is a structural gap rather than a considered accommodation of non-state autonomy.
reactions.condition_to_change: Opposition would soften only if the reform included an explicit, durable opt-out or negotiated exemption preserving existing accord-protected institutions' structural form, rather than proceeding to national conversion regardless of maintainer consent.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Same accord-based autonomy interest as above: a scenario that leaves selection age, track structure, and admissions untouched, and directs new funding and support mechanisms at general (predominantly state) schools, does not engage the autonomy the church-state agreements protect.
reactions.interest: Church and foundation (non-state) school maintainers' interest in institutional continuity and non-interference
reactions.public_good_frame: Stability of the existing plural structure allows each maintainer type to pursue its own mission without disruption, while resourcing gaps elsewhere in the system are addressed without requiring every maintainer to be restructured to fix them.
reactions.argument: This is the only scenario that neither imposes admissions constraints nor caps nor structural conversion on non-state maintainers; it addresses SES-linked inequity by investing in disadvantaged general schools rather than by regulating or dismantling our institutions. That said, the mechanism includes 'rigorous, standardized national curriculum expectations... across schools regardless of track,' which is stated broadly enough that it could be read to reach denominational schools' pedagogical and religious-education content, an area the accords place within our autonomy.
reactions.condition_to_change: Support would turn to opposition if the national curriculum standardization component were applied to denominational schools in a way that overrides religious-education content or restricts our accord-protected pedagogical distinctiveness, rather than being scoped to general (state) schools as the scenario's stated target.
